hero.eyebrow: Medical Sales · Goldman Sachs Alum · Licensed EMT · DFW
hero.name: Kelsey
hero.tagline: Built across missions, boardrooms, and trauma bays.

narrative.title: How do you just make things happen?
narrative.body:
People have asked me my whole life how I just make things happen. I never had a good answer until recently.

As a Spanish speaking missionary across three states, I inherited the territory everyone before me had written off. I built it into our most productive account through one relationship that unlocked everything. A corporate career that took me from Vivint Solar, where I identified a $2M opportunity and sold it to senior leadership, to Goldman Sachs, where my hiring manager called my interview one of the best he had ever seen. Most recently, finishing my EMT program in the top 5% of my class and finding myself in emergency rooms and trauma bays executing under pressure alongside physicians.

Different rooms. Same instinct. Read the person. Understand what they need. Earn their trust. Find a way to close the gap. That is what I have been doing my entire life. In medical sales it just finally has a name.

startStories.title: The Territory Everyone Wrote Off
startStories.teaser: I was assigned a territory where the key stakeholder had already said no to reps before me, and the numbers showed it.
startStories.body:
My job was to find people willing to join our organization. Cold calling strangers rarely worked, warm referrals did. Most members weren't reps like me, they were local families who met multiple times a week. In each area, one key stakeholder connected reps to those members. Mine had shut out every rep before me, and the territory's numbers had suffered for it.

I didn't push. My companion and I got to know his wife first, no agenda, just showing up over time. Eventually she mentioned he missed playing pickup soccer. That was my opening. We organized a game. He came. We kept it going. He went from closed off to actively referring members and trusting us with his network.

That territory became one of the strongest in the district.

What that taught me is a no isn't the end, it just means you haven't found the right angle yet. Trust can't be rushed. That's exactly what it takes with a physician who's already said no to reps before.

startStories.title: The Goldman Standard
startStories.teaser: At Goldman I managed multi million dollar loan portfolios where one missed detail in a hundred page contract meant real money and real trust on the line.
startStories.body:
I managed a portfolio of institutional clients across multi million dollar syndicated loan accounts. The contracts behind those loans ran hundreds of pages of dense legal language, and a rate change buried at one specific point in the contract could not be missed. I learned to read those contracts front to back so I'd catch a discrepancy before it ever reached a client.

When clients had questions I answered in plain terms, not legal speak, because their trust in me depended on actually understanding what I told them. I kept every deadline I gave a client, including working from a plane on Christmas Eve once, because the commitment mattered more than the inconvenience. When teammates were out I picked up their accounts too, so no client ever felt a gap in service.

I was the face of Goldman to those clients. That meant white glove service and 100% accuracy at the same time, no exceptions.

That role taught me accuracy and warmth aren't a tradeoff. A client has to trust you're never wrong on the details and that you value their time as much as your own deadlines. That's exactly what a physician expects from a rep.

startStories.title: The Rooftop Trauma Night
startStories.teaser: Three months into my EMT program, an emergency air transport put me in a trauma bay with a team I'd never worked with before, and no time to think about it.
startStories.body:
I was three months into my EMT program, about 2am during a 12 hour overnight shift. It had already been an intense night. My mentor pulled me mid task and told me to follow two nurses immediately.

We ran to the rooftop helipad to receive an emergency air transport. The nurses taught me the protocol on the way up. We received the patient and raced him through the hospital into the trauma bay.

The surgeon came in. A couple of EMT students were in the room. He barked a command to cut the patient's clothes off with shears. I stepped forward immediately and started cutting while the surgeon performed what I later identified as an emergency tracheotomy. The nurses looked over their shoulders and told the other students who had frozen to follow my lead.

What that night taught me is you don't rise to the occasion, you fall to your level of preparation and instinct. The team that trusts its training moves together without anyone having to think twice, whether that's a trauma bay or a physician's office.
